frontier: GNFS constant-factor ledger: Can the d=5 nonzero-landing curve construction (496 bits, N^0.10–0.13 height) advance beyond univariate GNFS, and does Riemann-Roch relation extraction preserve its height advantage?
action:
Milestone: next_experiment_design; contract=sha256:8f51055608cd87448dc0465787ec0c29684f1bdb3c8d9d4e4f45ef8ef7c42a8c

Structured next-experiment design for R3 (Sage/Magma function-field NFS relation extraction on validated d=5 genus-6 curves) plus minimal structural replay of Probe H: 20 trials of sparse bivariate support sampling (d=5, |A|=14 from 21 monomials) confirmed structural precondition met in 14/20 cases (70%), non-automatic.

result: Next-experiment design completed. R3 requires Sage/Magma (unavailable). Probe H structural precondition confirmed: sparse bivariate support selection matters — not all sparse supports yield sufficient leading-DOF for factorization (14/20 with seed=42). Full Probe H verification requires fpylll; full R3 requires Sage/Magma. Route confirmed closed at probe scale pending environment change.
next_gate: Install fpylll and run full Probe H: LLL-reduce modular constraint lattice on 3 degree-5 sparse supports, factor leading forms with sympy, record factorization frequency. Or install SageMath and execute R3 directly on d=5 genus-6 curves.
boundary_held: No production Atlas writes, external submissions, git pushes, or training.

frontier_consult.outcome: Sage/Magma and fpyllf unavailable; structural replay ran instead (sympy-only)
